[IMAGE:0]It's a little past midnight, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will not be put through.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

(The image? I've finished the sequence of my published books; while I decide what I want to do next, I have some memes to share.)

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Return to Sender?
comments.content:
Is there an ethical form of a "Return to Sender" spell that I can use?

I am afflicted by bill collectors. This is no debt I've acquired by any misdeed or choice of my own, but is the result of a medical misfortune that has befallen a member of my family and that affects the rest of us.

One of these bill collectors has repeatedly threatened my very elderly mother and myself with total ruin in quite an evil way. I am doing all I can to resolve the matter on the material plane, but while doing so I wish to be free from such threats, which are an unpleasant distractant from the many necessary tasks. What, if anything, can I do?

My magical background is Golden Dawn, Hellenic style. But I'm prepared to use any tradition that will work, so long as it be ethical and produce no blowback.

replies.title: Re: Return to Sender?
replies.content: Invoke Saturn/Kronos. He is the lord of debts and their repayment, and he is also the lord of strict retributive justice and hard limits. He has no patience with those who demand more than their due. Ask him to bind this bill collector so that he cannot harm you or your mother, and to assist you in paying off your just debts.

replies.title: Re: Return to Sender?
replies.content: Thank you! My natal Saturn is somewhat afflicted, but I don't think I'll let that stop me in this case. Should I use the teachings of Israel Regardie in this respect, as in "The Art of True Healing," or rather use the Orphic Hymn to Saturn, with appropriate incense? Or perhaps also his invoking hexagram? And should I wait until Saturday, or will it be enough to go with Saturn hour, or perhaps while Saturn is culminating?

replies.title: Re: Return to Sender?
replies.content: Why not do both? Regardie's method can be used every day -- persistence and patience are Saturnine qualities, and he respects those who practice them -- and the Orphic hymn (with hexagram if you like) should be done in the day and hour of Saturn. See if you can find some myrrh incense -- that's a good Saturnine incense.

replies.title: Re: Return to Sender?
replies.content: As an additional option Frankincense works for Saturn too. I forgot the source but it was quoted as incense of the gods, and Saturn was mentioned.

replies.title: Re: Return to Sender?
replies.content: You can always burn frankincense to any of the gods. Myrrh is more specific.

replies.title: Re: Return to Sender?
replies.content: What a wretch! Probably following company policy, they unleash their hellhounds — "we don't care how you get the money."

comments.title: Regarding (Ogham) divinations
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Are divinations always completely honest? Or is it possible to get an incorrect divination in order for the petitioner to change his or her future behavior and actions so that the petitioner in the future does not become the recipient of a bad result (that would be due to not changing and staying the same)?

I'm sorry if this is a stupid question; I attribute it to being in denial. Upon receipt of a recent divination, my brain wanted to believe (in no particular order):
-Well, it will be true if I don’t change x.
-It is true now, but it can change to false if I do y.
-It’s lying to me to get me extremely motivated to change my habits now because I am naturally lazy or slow to action.
-I need to stop asking Ogham questions where I want a yes or no answer.

I am asking this question because of my concern for a particular Ogham divination, but I would like to broaden the scope of the question to any other relevant divination system.

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: Regarding (Ogham) divinations
replies.content: That is to say, you really don't want to hear what the divination told you. That does happen -- and the lesson to learn here is never to ask the oracle a question if you can't handle the answer.

Hi JMG,

After reading your answer and re-reading what I wrote, I'm embarrassed that I still posted my question; I've read your writings for a long time, I believe since 2008, and I have listened to some podcasts you've been a guest of, and my denial mind still thought you might say something different.

So mostly for my benefit and to have this as a short memorandum, since the first step to fixing a problem is admitting there is one, I admit I have a problem.

I believe the problem is, I've lived most of my life thinking I'd never have to pay for the consequences of my earlier, sometimes very stupid, actions and behavior.

And for anyone reading this thread who wishes to add their 2 cents (with or without salt and pepper and Tabasco sauce, etc.), please do so with my blessing.

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: Step 1

No need to kick yourself about having been suckered into believing it; just let go of the mistaken belief, accept that every thought, word, and deed has consequences for good and evil, and go on with life.

comments.content:
Last week someone mentioned an impromptu prayer to Pele, and I would like to share my thoughts, as someone who knows more about Hawaii and the traditions of this part of the world. First, the sense of amusement is actually be a bad sign, because of one of Pele’s lesser known habits: she is widely known to claim everything volcanic on the Island of Hawaii without fail, but she also reserves the right to claim anything that touches her island, or draws her attention in any way shape or form. This includes people: such people will have remarkably good lives if they remain on her island, but she does not like anything or anyone that amuses her leaving, and so leaving the island, especially without a plan to return, is dangerous once you’ve drawn her attention.

According to a few of the people who still practice the old religion that I have spoken to, there was a long standing belief that once you catch Pele’s attention, in any way, she almost always expects you to either come to her island at once, of if already there, to remain on her island for the rest of your life. As such, my advice for the person who did the prayer is that if you are already off the island, the moment anything happens that seems to suggest you should return to Hawaii, go. Don't ask questions: don't try to figure out how to make it work: just go.

I’d also strongly suggest that if you are still on the island, do some divination to find out if she is willing to let you leave, and if you have already left, get some divination to find out if you need to return ASAP; sometimes she'll be patient and wait for you to get the hint; but sometimes the way you find out you annoyed her is a very sudden and very violent death.

This also makes the Island of Hawaii itself a dangerous place to visit, so I cringe whenever I here tourists talking about going there: some fraction of the people who visit will be interesting to Pele, and while they can live remarkable lives if they stay on her island because there is nothing quite like a goddess pouring blessings into every aspect of your life, most of them will leave, and from everything I know, she really does not like that. I do not envy the people left with a choice between leaving everyone and everything they've ever known, and having a powerful god angry at them for the rest of their life: and especially because it can happen to children who in some cases won't even be old enough to remember the trip.

Pele is most emphatically not a safe deity to make contact with, and I wonder sometimes if the issues with tourists visiting her island and then leaving might have something to do with some of the issues the world faces right now: it would not surprise me to learn a fairly larger percentage of the comfortable classes has annoyed her by coming to her island, catching her attention, and then leaving....

OP here:

It is only if you succeed in catching her interest in a deeper way than a momentary interest that she might decide you need to come to Hawaii. I should have said that it is only if you catch her attention in a religious fashion that she expects you to come to her island, but it is anything religious that could do it, even something extremely minor.

If the traditional understandings I have are right, there isn't any risk to anyone from reading these comments: the most that might happen is that she might come take a look and decide she doesn't like what I'm saying. Anyone foolish enough to pray to her, or use devotional images of her, or otherwise interact with her in a religious fashion, is taking a huge risk unless they have a plan to go the Island of Hawaii if they do succeed in catching her attention; but talking about her is, provided it is respectful, pretty safe.

comments.title: 3 questions about the Sphere of Protection
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Preface:
I am giving it another try at learning and practicing the SoP.

You posted 8 Phases, the 1st being the opening and closing, and the other 7 being the 7 elemental phases. But I am interested in the fact that you spaced each post/homework/practice about a week apart.
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/tag/sphere+of+protection

Question 1:
Is one week optimal for each Phase? Or should I practice one Phase until I feel comfortable enough, and then move onto the next Phase?

I am assuming that 7 practices per Phase won't result in the particular Phase being really effective. I am basing this off of a paragraph from your Phase 1 post:

"The earlier version of the closing, the actual establishment of the Sphere of Protection, involved some fairly complicated visualizations. Those work, but a lot of people have had trouble with them. The following visualization will be found less challenging. Fair warning, though: it needs regular practice to become really effective."

Question 2:
Also, would it be permissible for me as a Roman Catholic to say 2 prayers at the end of the closing: the Lord’s Prayer and one Hail Mary? Or just stick to one prayer?

Question 3:
I don't remember the exact wording you used, but the gist was that magic should not be practiced (or should be limited) when the person is currently suffering from (severe) mental illness like (severe) clinical depression. In such cases (if I remembered correctly), I presume that the advice to refrain from magic does not apply to the Octagon Society lessons; but what about practicing the Sphere of Protection, as well as performing a "cut and clear" spell?

Thank you,
-E.

replies.title: Re: 3 questions about the Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
1) One week is enough time to learn each step, so you can do it from memory. Of course you'll keep on getting better at it as you continue. I've been practicing the SoP for a little over twenty years now and I'm still seeing improvements in its effect.

2) You can certainly do those, or any other prayers you like.

3) If you're heavily medicated, it's probably not a good time to do ceremonial magic beyond the basics, but a daily SoP is perfectly safe and will help you. Depression generally isn't a problem -- it's the various forms of schizophrenia that can be problematic. The Octagon Society work can be done by anyone, and it's especially helpful for depression -- though it can be a rough road, since depression is usually a tertiary emotion; under it is anger, and under the anger is some combination of fear, grief, and shame, all of which you have to experience and release before you get out from under the depression. As for a cut and clear spell, that should be fine as well.

replies.title: Re: Witches
replies.content:
The word that King James's scholars translated "witch" in the original Hebrew text is mekashephah. It probably means "herb cutter" and thus "poisoner" -- the Septuagint, the translation of the Jewish scriptures into Hellenistic Greek made in ancient times when early Hebrew was better understood, translated the word as pharmakeia, "herbalist." Biblical Hebrew had other words meaning "witch, sorceress," and the like; they don't appear here.

Furthermore, the verb translated "to live" in the English text, khayah, means "to live, to flourish, to reside, to dwell among." The text does not say "you shall put a mekhashephah to death," which it says relentlessly in many, many other passages. The implication is that the issue is letting the mekhashephah live among the Israelites, not that she should be put to death.

So it should come out, "You shall not permit a poisoner to live among you." That's very sensible advice, of course.

comments.title: Pyramids in Cyberspace
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I was thinking about how many of today's tech moguls have a stated goal of immortality and how their ambitions run parallel to similar efforts in the past. The old approach to achieving some form of life after death was to perform an elaborate burial and require devotees to make regular offerings of food and drink to the deceased. It seems like this could sustain some aspect of a person's consciousness after their death.

Silicon Valley CEOs generally have a materialistic view of the world and desire the indefinite continuity of their flesh and blood, but at some point it's likely they'll be faced with physical decay that no nutrients or drugs can stave off. I imagine at that point they'll be willing to entertain any possible method for continued existence, even if it's disembodied. For someone who controls a website visited by billions of people or another type of critical computer infrastructure, the logical method could be to construct a "pyramid in cyberspace," some kind of system used by many people that elicits their devotion to the deceased person. People posting on a social media app could be faced with an unskippable ad telling them to "take a moment to thank So-and-So for making this possible." This could be combined with some kind of elaborate tomb and maybe even a priesthood who perform rites for the former captain of industry.

Do you think such a system is feasible? The old rites for the dead focused on providing them with straightforward human needs: food, drink and housing. Modern electronic communication makes it possible to steer behavior at a much larger scale, but the votive acts made thereby would probably be less tangible than traditional food offerings. Still, with billions of people involved I would guess the spirits of these dead could be invested with much more power than the pharaohs of old. Do you agree?

One other question: CEOs are already compared to vampires and a number of elites are already getting blood transfusions from younger people in a bid to avoid aging. I heard one of them is using his own son as a blood bank for maximum compatibility. Do you think these practices make it more likely that they will continue as vampiric spirits after death? You've written that the burden of caring for the deceased sparked a "rebellion of the living against the dead" in many ancient cultures, but I could see these factors leading to a much stronger tyranny of the dead than anything seen in the ancient world.

replies.title: Re: Pyramids in Cyberspace
replies.content:
1) Oh, they might try it. Given the very low esteem in which the current crop of tech overlords are held in by most people these days, though, my guess is that it would implode promptly, as most of the people who got that ad would say something like "F*** Bill Gates," especially if the ad was annoying (as of course it would be), and the magically trained might go out of their way to trace a banishing pentagram over it, or even recite a ritual of exorcism.

2) No, it would be a much weaker tyranny, because the technical tricks of generating and preserving an etheric revenant (the proper term for what we're discussing) were mostly lost by the time Rome fell, and have been very imperfectly recovered. Vampirism as such is simply a last-ditch attempt to preserve the old etheric revenant tradition once you could no longer set up the kind of gimmick that Egyptian pharaohs could count on, with a competent initiatory priesthood doing all the necessary rites -- and even that was a failed attempt to pervert a much older and saner tradition, which wasn't about prolonging the postmortem existence of dead tyrants.

Back in the age of the long barrows, people from certain family bloodlines voluntarily went into the barrows after a long period of fasting and spiritual exercises, and maintained themselves in a meditative state while their material bodies died of hypothermia and thirst. In this way they became guardian spirits of their tribes, stepping out of the cycle of reincarnation for a time, until their bones were taken out of the barrows and exposed to sun and wind on hilltop enclosures, and a new volunteer went into the earth. (The legends of Merlin's burial in the crystal cave include distant echoes of this tradition.) Millennia later, after new peoples moved into those lands, new religious beliefs emerged and so did feudal hierarchies, the long barrows gave way to round barrows in which kings and lords were buried, and the tradition fell into decadence. Vampirism in China and eastern Europe was the last remnant of that tradition, and it was stamped out with extreme prejudice for very good reason.

replies.title: Re: Pyramids in Cyberspace
replies.content:
1) That's a weird choice of thing to worry about, if taken at face value. If you remove the materialism, a lot of the reasons for going so hard on a search for immortality break down, in such a way that it takes huge amount of time and subcultural immersion and rolls of the dice for someone to end up with some new structure of reasons for searching for immortality that looks anything like the sort of structure that the Egyptian pharaohs or random Chinese imperial officeholders had. Non-materialist end-of-life tech CEOs will tend to be Christians, Buddhists, or Hindus; that's already one roll of the dice that knocks out almost all of the hypothetical candidates from the get-go.

(JMG: I suppose you used Bill Gates as your example because he was party to a system that was so high-handed in relation to the vaccines, but it's kind of dissonant with what I know of the guy, especially since his imposition of will in the matter of the vaccines involved him believing himself to be making the world a better place, whereas it would be improbable for him to hold together a legitimizing narrative like that in the context of generalized immortality, especially since he's vaguely Christian-adjacent. Mark Zuckerberg would be a better candidate, since he gets weird monomaniacal-perhaps-sociopathic focuses on gaining control in more general forms, without so much of a constraint as to whether it's making the world a better place, and he gets his ideas about what kinds of control to be monomaniacal about gaining from random non-mainstream sometimes poorly-informed idiosyncratic ways of viewing what's possible in the world.)

Of course the real thing to worry about / look ahead to, in relation to immortality and network-scale power, is whole-brain emulation, or some approximation thereunto. But that gets into this whole cosmological can of worms, where apparently the Church-Turing thesis (governing the relevance of such scenarios) is an externally-enforced "scissor statement", where both the believers and the disbelievers of the Church-Turing thesis are effectively in different jaggedly-smashed-into-each-other universes where they get different evidences (via distinctions which turn upon very subtle matters of interpretation and of how to extrapolate or not extrapolate patterns from clearer-cut instances of delusion), and where most of the partisans of either side, when pressed, don't understand how the people on the other side could have legitimately arrived at their conclusions in good faith from the evidence that they "must" have had.

replies.title: Re: Pyramids in Cyberspace
replies.content:
No, I used Bill Gates because he's one of the best examples of a tech magnate who's never noticed that the vast majority of people who have to deal with his corporation's products loathe the company and despise him personally. He's got an image of himself as global benefactor, and could probably talk himself into believing that becoming immortal would let him keep on helping the world.

Whole-brain emulation would be amusing, in a very bleak sense. They could unquestionably make a computer that imitated Gates's verbal output, and quite possibly even one that lurched through some simulacrum of his thinking processes. Just as large language models aren't actually intelligent -- they simply produce statistically likely strings of words that imitate human communication, a whole-brain emulator would imitate the soul of the person...while that soul went spinning off into the afterlife and whatever fate his thoughts, words, and deeds made for him.

replies.title: Re: Pyramids in Cyberspace
replies.content:
I would have said something that was factorized into causal elements in a different way, and might lead to different predictions. Like, my guess is that the influence of the soul works through Something that picks which timelines of material-world details and outcomes are going to get (more) experienced or (more) not-experienced, as being relevant to souls' choices and attention-habits. It'd be up to the discretion of that "Something" whether it judged a brain emulation as a suitable focus for its influence, to effect whatever its purposes were in relation to those souls.

If the planes are in part the tiers of a drafting process for material-world-outcome-like experiences, then the absence of an etheric layer of the drafting process in the brain-emulation might already cause the "Something" to judge the brain-emulation to be too inconvenient to be cost-effective to engage with on its own level for any real length of time. I mean, PK experiments on deterministic random number generators did seem to get more or less the same kinds of results, presumably by fated boundary conditions of randomized initial state, as the experiments on generators with inherent physical randomness did. And that means it wouldn't be inherently impossible for the "Something" to pick fated boundary conditions for the brain-emulation machinery in the same way, to produce more or less the same conditional-probability-distribution steering as it would have if the soul had been attached in the more conventional way. But the existence of those experimental findings doesn't say much about the relative costs as weighed by the "Something" of controlling those boundary conditions, especially in more detail or over longer timespans.

In the absence of the selection-like steering of outcomes by that "Something" on behalf of the souls it's being responsive to, the brain-emulation would be in some sense untethered, and might end up falling back on... Well, this part isn't clear, actually. If there was was just a simulated-etheric problem -- if ongoing etheric interactions are necessary for a person not to, say, immediately get the equivalent of extreme radiation sickness and then metabolically implode a couple hours later, then that necessity might turn up in cell-level first-principles simulations, and be put down to unexpected many-particle interaction effects not covered by the electron density functional approximation or something, which could maybe be tweaked and fudged until those etheric interactions were adequately substituted for. (This would already in some sense be a positive finding in a parapsychology experiment, but conceivably the "Something" could go on for a long time finding ways to finesse the discrepancy and keep it from mass awareness -- at least, so long as the mathematical toolset involved in the engineering of the physical-chemistry simulation never got too good.) But what would be the equivalent "imitation" of steering soul-coupling, that would be generated by a purely bottom-up simulation of neural activity without the ordinarily observed top-down stuff?

My guess is that what you'd see, you wouldn't call an "imitation" of a soul; faithfully reproducing all the bottom-up effects while leaving out the top-down effects would instead just result in the equivalent of a completely passive soul permanently stuck in neutral gear. The emulated brain would be able to think and learn, but it might lack inspirations. If it had temporal-lobe seizures, they would never be divine. It might more readily get stuck in a thousand and one little ruts and obsessions and rabbit-holes, that even only just a normally healthy soul normally directs the mind away from as a waste of time. In the etheric case, there would have been a bunch of knobs and dials in the parameters of the electron-density functional or whatever that could be twiddled until you got an imitation of etheric support, since the etheric effects would exist at more or less the same level of organization as the quantum chemistry that the parameters being twiddled were at. (Unless etheric effects substantially work at the level of gene regulation or something! But that would be too detectable, right?) But when it comes to inspiration and self-possessedness, you couldn't get the effects of a soul by frowning at your neuron-level approximations and twiddling their parameters to match reductionist experiments, because the soul would be acting at a higher level of organization.

I don't know. Maybe with some work, you could tune the neurons to occasionally produce a sort of generalization of temporal-lobe seizures that had random inspiration-like effects, but where the inspirations ultimately proved (at least in the eyes of everyone else) to be disorganized and empty false inspirations much more often than the natural kind. That would be more like what you probably meant by speaking of imitation of the soul.

That would still run into the problem of how e.g. the discrepancy between rates of empty vs. non-empty inspirations between natural brains and emulated ones would maybe constitute a positive finding in a parapsychology experiment. But there'd be so much else going on in the differences between real brains and emulated ones that it would take a lot of work to get proper experimental controls, which might be what it would take to make that into a stark discrepancy that people wouldn't be left with any way to interpret-away.

As for your claim that brain emulations would definitely strongly attract demons -- are there other sources of inductive/abductive/whatever evidence you can point to for study, besides those that you allude to in "The Subnatural Realm: A Hypothesis" (i.e.: Steiner's visionary worldview and predictions; some cosmological-mythological associations of size; C.S. Lewis's argument about apparent physical causal non-closure in (a single-world version of) quantum mechanics; and the way that that digital technologies supposedly lend themselves more to Ahrimanic vices than Luciferian ones)? These seem pretty tentative evidence by themselves. The only other items of evidence that I can think of myself off the top of my head are:

- There was some some Eastern tradition of landscaping where evil spirits find straight lines more congenial while good spirits are much better at handling paths with curves and corners. I'm not entirely sure how to draw the dots from there but it would loosely go along with the theme.

- The Abramelin book describes an operation which produces magic squares having a dream-like symbolic resemblance to the deep-learning vector-transformation matrices produced in large language or other media model training, and these squares do not seem to differentiate between angelic and demonic invocations. That's not as directly relevant as it could be, because emulated synapses are more complicated than numbers in matrices, and more irregular in their connections than the way that the all the neatly arranged rows of a matrix connect to all the neatly arranged columns.

- The report in the other thread about how blessing the coolant water in a machine running one of Stability AI's image-generation models shortly preceded the coolant pump stalling and the electronics being destroyed by overheating. This feels weirdly intuitive to me, too, but I wonder whether it's some variant of a celestial-vs.-telluric thing rather than a straightforwardly divine-vs.-demonic one, like not sacrificing pork to Olympians.

replies.title: Re: Pyramids in Cyberspace
replies.content:
No, that won't wash. Human incarnation isn't just a matter of plugging a soul into some randomly chosen physical matrix. The material body is only one of four vestures -- the others are etheric, astral, and mental -- and the soul interfaces with all four of them. Furthermore, it's not just in contact with the brain, it interfaces with the whole of each of its four vestures. Finally, the process of incarnation is under the supervision of spiritual beings, who aren't especially interested in the opinions of arrogant technocrats.

As for the likelihood that attempted whole-brain emulators will attract demons, it's a speculation, but a tolerably well grounded one. The interface between the subnatural realm and the demonic plane is shown in the weird way that LLMs tend to bias toward malevolence, in the moral degradation that seems to affect the whole internet, and in a great many examples like the one you cited.

comments.title: Dream: Fourteen Stations
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I hope you're feeling less tired now. Three Mondays ago, I mentioned a dream, probably born from frustrations with the GSF. While I have, as described in that comment, elected not to follow its call, it still bugs me that I don't really understand its meaning, especially as some symbolism feels oddly specific. Thus, I'd like to ask you and the commentariat for assistance.

The relevant fragment involves me standing in a rocky desert, possibly in conversation with somebody. I just received the first lesson of a correspondence course in some magical/occult system. I'm fairly certain that the outside said something about the Golden Dawn with a modifier (as in Celtic Golden Dawn or Heathen Golden Dawn).

The letter has an odd shape: an ellipse whose ends have been embiggened, given a pointy tip, and somewhat curved, like a claw. Imagine the visible part of a human eye (so everything between the lids) and twist the left corner downwards and the right corner upwards. This is the inside of the paper when it is unfolded; it was delivered with the corners folded inwards.

Displayed on the inside is another ellipse, diagonally with its left point up. Along the ellipse are fourteen stations (that's the wording from the dream) making up the path of whatever system this course is about. Each station follows the previous and the first follows the last. Several neighbouring stations are also grouped together into a bigger stage (unsure if that's the original wording) but I don't remember how many stages there are or if they are always comprised of the same amount of stations.

Each lesson has practices for six days and the lessons are sent out weekly; the teachers of the course "trust that students won't mind a day of rest." I wondered whether I would get the new lessons in a timely fashion, living in Europe and not the US.

That's all I remember. It's certainly possible that my subjective mind simply wanted to communicate "you're clearly frustrated, just try something else." What bothers me with that interpretation is that the whole fourteen station symbolism is both remarkably specific and not something I would naturally associate with occultism in general or the Golden Dawn in particular—I'd mostly associate the latter with the number ten, for probably obvious reasons. Plus, journaling on the day after the dream revealed an indifference to particular systems, including the GSF, as long as I committed to one and followed it.

Does any of the above ring a bell? Was this a pointer to a real occult system or just a fabrication of a frustrated unconscious?

My natural association with fourteen stations would be those of the cross. A search for fourteen stations in occultism has turned up a site apparently associated with Mark and Elisabeth Prophet's Church Universal and Triumphant but their website is giving me rather unpleasant vibes—plus, there's the whole failed doomsday prediction and attempting to call down a soviet nuclear strike on the US thing.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Dream: Fourteen Stations
replies.content:
Regardless, thank you for this space and your time.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: Dream: Fourteen Stations
replies.content:
Hi David P.

Three things come to mind. The first is how the Tree of Life is sometimes seen in vision as a desert in one school of magic I know of (Quareia course). The second is that in Franz Bardon's books, he suggests taking off a day, possibly two days, from magical practices, like one would from a normal job. Having at least a day off, while not something I personally prefer (my practices tend to charge me up and I like that... though sometimes a specific working drains and tires for a day or so). The third thing is the number 14, which to me is approx half a lunar cycle in either the waxing or the waning phase. Those ellipsis and stations starting and stopping might be the continuous nature of full moon to new moon to full moon.

If this were my dream I'd think of it less as a correspondence course from an existing school, and something from the astral plane you are picking up on, and might be a teaching that is thus "embedded" within you and will take a series of lunar cycles to gestate and unfold.

JPM -your friendly dream interpreter

comments.title: Rhetoric for the Renaissance Man and Questions on Amalgamata
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I enjoyed your latest appearance on Plant Cunning and found your discussion of amalgamata (spelling? Some quick web searches couldn't confirm I'm talking about the right thing) especially interesting, though it left me with a few questions.

To Share: I shared on the main blog on the latest open post, but for anyone who didn't see it, I've launched a new blog: Rhetoric for the Renaissance Man. On it, I'm going to share some of what I've learned about persuasive communication, but within a framework of setting goals in accordance with your values, pursuing them ethically, and generally taking an "old-fashioned" holistic approach to such things. Besides speaking and writing well, I plan on touching on defining your values, translating those into virtues you can live by, setting goals that accord with those values, becoming productive in pursuing those goals, and learning things from multiple disciplines to gain a wider, more nuanced view of your interests than a more specialized approach might bring. To make it relevant to MM, I basically view persuasion as a more mundane approach to "causing changes in consciousness in accordance with will," than we typically discuss here, but most modern approaches don't take the ethical dimension as seriously as I've come to appreciate about the Ecosophian approach, so I'd like to remedy that.

If any of that sounds interesting to you, check out my introduction to the blog here: https://rhetoricfortherenaissanceman.com/2025/09/24/laying-the-groundwork/

To Ask: In the discussion, you talked about how amalgamata, statues of the Gods stuffed with astrologically-corresponding materials, are like a specialized kind of talisman. I'm struck by the idea of incorporating some into my prayer practice, and I know you explain their use in an upcoming book, but thought I might ask a couple of questions in case I'm able to get started sooner:

1. Does the general advice for astrological talismans to avoid talismans invoking a planet afflicted in your birth chart apply to amalgamata, or does the divine influence moderate and overcome the tendency to call in the difficult energies the affliction indicates?
2. This might be a question to be settled by experimentation, but to the degree you have an opinion, if crafting statues for non-Greco-Roman deities, would you recommend trying to find corresponding herbs, minerals, et cetera from the relevant tradition, or trying to "map" the relevant deity onto one of the planets, and then choosing correspondences based on that? For example, if I were to create an amalgama of Odin, would I want to search the Heathen lore for Odinic correspondences, and include those, or just accept the Interpretatio Romana and choose a number of Mercurial correspondences?
3. You mentioned a ceremony to consecrate the amalgamata and a few details. Would the following be a workable way to get started with using them in prayer: Wait until the day and hour of the closest-equivalent planet to the God/dess, open the holy space of the altar, place the statue on it, light incense appropriate to the deity, say a brief prayer invoking the God/dess and asking Him/Her to be present and bring His/Her energies into your life, pause to "listen" for a response, and when satisfied, close the holy space and leave the statue there for regular worship.
4. Lastly, if you are forced to make use of a temporary altar that must at times be taken down, is there any special treatment recommended for the amalgamata when closing/taking down the altar and putting them away?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and those who read and comment.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

comments.title: Goddess of Fermentation: Ninkasi
comments.content:
Someone asked about Gods of Fermentation. Ninkasi of Mesopotamia is One. There is a Hymn to Her that actually describes how to brew beer.

The following translation of the Hymn to Ninkasi is by Miguel Civil:

Hymn to Ninkasi

Borne of the flowing water,
Tenderly cared for by the Ninhursag,
Borne of the flowing water,
Tenderly cared for by the Ninhursag,

Having founded your town by the sacred lake,
She finished its great walls for you,
Ninkasi, having founded your town by the sacred lake,
She finished its walls for you,

Your father is Enki, Lord Nidimmud,
Your mother is Ninti, the queen of the sacred lake.
Ninkasi, your father is Enki, Lord Nidimmud,
Your mother is Ninti, the queen of the sacred lake.

You are the one who handles the dough [and] with a big shovel,
Mixing in a pit, the bappir with sweet aromatics,
Ninkasi, you are the one who handles the dough [and] with a big shovel,
Mixing in a pit, the bappir with [date] - honey,

You are the one who bakes the bappir in the big oven,
Puts in order the piles of hulled grains,
Ninkasi, you are the one who bakes the bappir in the big oven,
Puts in order the piles of hulled grains,

You are the one who waters the malt set on the ground,
The noble dogs keep away even the potentates,
Ninkasi, you are the one who waters the malt set on the ground,
The noble dogs keep away even the potentates,

You are the one who soaks the malt in a jar,
The waves rise, the waves fall.
Ninkasi, you are the one who soaks the malt in a jar,
The waves rise, the waves fall.

You are the one who spreads the cooked mash on large reed mats,
Coolness overcomes,
Ninkasi, you are the one who spreads the cooked mash on large reed mats,
Coolness overcomes,

You are the one who holds with both hands the great sweet wort,
Brewing [it] with honey [and] wine
(You the sweet wort to the vessel)
Ninkasi, (...)(You the sweet wort to the vessel)

The filtering vat, which makes a pleasant sound,
You place appropriately on a large collector vat.
Ninkasi, the filtering vat, which makes a pleasant sound,
You place appropriately on a large collector vat.

When you pour out the filtered beer of the collector vat,
It is [like] the onrush of Tigris and Euphrates.
Ninkasi, you are the one who pours out the filtered beer of the collector vat,
It is [like] the onrush of Tigris and Euphrates.

comments.content:
On the topic of a potential cultural backlash against any and all forms of alternative spirituality, one thing I pointed out in an above comment is just how terrible the PR that's come from these movements have been for quite some time.

I know you've commented before about how much of the Neopagan movement and pop-occult scene became so heavily fixated on playing their designated role as the "baddies" of the Christian mythical universe, rather than practicing any sort of constructive spirituality. There seems to be so much truth to this observation. A comment from Jennifer on last Wed's open post really hit the nail on the head, IMHO:

"...it occurs to me that one step toward not freaking out devout Christians would be to stop putting images of dead animal heads, demons weeping blood, creepy animated jack-o-lanterns, and other evil-looking stuff on pagan logos, ads, and merch. Especially if you’re staging an event in a rural county in Texas!

Alternatively, with the level of political discourse these days, maybe we could just start a Good Normal People Club and whenever anyone suggests we might be up to no good, we can just point them to the club name."

I've had similar ideas in recent years, that there needs to be a Normal People Club of some type that can attempt to curate alt-spiritual discourse in a positive direction and provide much more charitable PR than the garbage optics which has been the norm for too long.

One big problem I see is that occult movements have for centuries been joined at the hip with secularizing and liberalizing movements and cultural tendencies throughout the history of the West (for obvious and understandable reasons, tbh), thus the constant suspicion from conservatives and traditionalists that anything esoteric must be tied up with this or that political conspiracy or subversive group (Early Freemasons facilitating the spread of liberal ideologies and many Theosophists moonlighting as socialists, are two major examples that come to mind). At the same time, alternatives to dogmatic mainstream Christianity have become quite normalized in a way that's never really happened prior to the modern era, so that could very well be a mitigating factor.

1. Do you think it's too late to right the ship? Is a simple condemnation of cursing and black magic enough for anyone looking to present these alternative traditions in a better light?

2. Would you agree that some of the existing terminology used (like "Witch" as a term of endearment rather than a strong pejorative) in these movements needs to be jettisoned moving forward? I think normies tend to respond to words primarily as emotional triggers more than conveyors of clear meaning; every marketing guru seems to know this quite well. BY that, I'd say that "branding" can be thought of as a rather potent form of magic.

Finally, for any discussions on this topic that goes past the midnight deadline, I have an ongoing thread on my blog that everyone is graciously invited to chime in on and perhaps spitball and brainstorm ideas related to the above:
https://causticus.dreamwidth.org/74744.html

comments.title: Eugene Fersen's Science of Being
comments.content:
Hello, JMG,

Science of Being – anon reporting. This week I’m finishing working through the first part of the Eugene Fersen’s correspondence course, dedicated to physical well-being and development, so this time I’m sharing a few notes on exercises covered so far:

Relaxation + Silence: rather self-explanatory, bread and butter of almost any system of occult training. As I’ve written before, I find the meditation posture Fersen teaches (a variant of Hieratic posture with the left palm facing up and the right palm facing down) conducive to relaxation. I’m not sure why it works that way, if I had to hazard a guess, I’d say it’s a conditioned response to relaxation exercise, since it is not a posture one accidentally adopts.

Contact with the Universal Life Energ: the crux of the Fersen’s system, it is achieved either through saying a certain phrase (mental contact) or adopting a certain posture (physical contact or Star Exercise). After the contact is established the energy can be redirected by willing it to flow through the fingers, the left hand being receptive, and the right hand being radiating. Again, not particularly new. Fersen prescribes using life energy for healing, as well as magnetizing items. Some of the examples he gives are bordering on infringing on other people’s free will, but he insist this is fine, because the Universal Life Energy can do no harm. Absent from the correspondence course version are the instructions for using magnetism to enhance one’s sleep, found in the book version. I’ve experimented with the sleep exercise a few times, since it is one of the key reasons I become interested in his system, but obtained no results so far.

Stimulation: an extension of the physical contact with the Universal Life Energy. I’m unsure if I performed that exercise right, since the instructions given were rather vague, but the way I understand it, it is supposed to be similar to the pore breathing exercises found in many other systems.

Invigoration and Contact with the Infinite Supply of All Wealth: both of those are variants of acting (namely, breathing) with intention, former is intended for obtaining health, latter for obtaining wealth. Fersen prescribes working with any given exercise for a week to familiarize yourself with it, and then putting it away until after finishing the course, unless specified otherwise, so I can’t really say how effective they are.

Rejuvenation: another extension of the physical contact, this time it involves willing energy to flow from solar plexus to various parts of the body with the intention of healing them. Again, fairly standard exercise, found in many other systems. It does produce the feeling of being rested, however.

What is different from other New Thought systems I’ve encountered so far is Fersen’s aversion to affirmations, as he warns against their use and denounces the practice as harmful abuse of the will. I'm not sure what to make of it.

Next week I’m starting the second part of the course, dedicated to mental well-being and development, so I plan on reporting again in a month on what it has to offer.

As always, thank you for hosting Magic Monday and best wishes to you and fellow posters.

replies.title: Re: Eugene Fersen's Science of Being
replies.content:
An occultist with an aversion to affirmations? How truly bizarre! I wonder what his personal interpretation of the term affirmation might be. Perhaps New Thought's unbridled enthusiasm for them left him unable to consider affirmations as a much broader category than New Thought's limited, branded application of them was encouraging.

As far as I can tell, any verbal attempt to exert one's will can be considered a kind of affirmation, regardless of how well or poorly executed it may be. "I call on the spirits above", "I ask God to work his will through me", "I invite Athena to guide me", and "I offer my blessings to every being as I pass" are all affirmations. Of course, "I'm not superstitious, so I don't believe in gods", "my family drives me crazy", "I'm bound to screw this up again", and "god damn it" are all affirmations of a different sort. Those who have rigorously practiced channeling their will will naturally obtain stronger results from any of those affirming attempts to influence their consciousness. As the saying goes, "practice makes perfect... so be careful what you practice", another useful affirmation boldly attempting to get reality to line up with our cherished expectations.

Whether thought of as a dialogue between one's consciousness and one's will or one's soul or a divinity, the broad term affirmation can easily include our heartfelt prayers, curses, rationalizations, and other intentional attempts to convince or align some influenceable power in our consciously desired direction. As in the Greek metaphor of a charioteer needing to learn how to bridle in and train his willful horses to pull his chariot in his desired direction, we all can benefit from learning how to guide, tame, and cooperate with our restless will.

There are also those wild occasions when we can abdicate conscious control altogether, such as in the passion of a creative frenzy or the super-sensory state of going berserker or in the deepest kind of trance. Outside of those rare occasions' initiating instant, when we may consciously cede control over oursleves to some higher power by using an affirmation like "I am getting out of the way now", those wild abdications are one of the only instances I know of when we stop employing affirmations altogether.

"I like this", "I don't like that", "this political party is inherently good/evil", "I'm bored", "he's stupid", "you can't make me" — who exactly are we saying these inner thoughts to? Who are we trying to convince that that's the corner we should get ourselves stuck in? Who temporarily stops repeating, all mantra-like, those preferred inanities when the genius/berserker magically manages to take hold? Affirmations could simply be the way we verbally interact with the universe and attempt to influence it, to our own advantage or detriment, as the case may be.

On the other hand, affirmations could also be regarded as a much smaller category of intentionally crafted and effortlessly repeated unfulfilled hopes and dreams, with an ample smear of entitlement and self-centeredness to render them thoroughly unappealing. Given their inherent potency, affirmations can certainly be twisted beyond all recognition into deadening anchors to keep us firmly tethered to our baser natures, as Janis Joplin so brilliantly parodied by droning “Oh Lord, won’t you buy me a color TV?” But who would want to drain affirmations of all their potency by relegating them to the orphaned category of also-rans? A clue: Corporate interests with a desire to profit off of captive consumers, ruthlessly gaslighted into believing in their own inherent impotency, would be one place to look for likely culprits!

With so many ways to abuse the will on offer in these perilous times, why would any occultist feel the need to single out one of the best keys to unlocking the door out of this dreary prison for his special warning and denunciation? Relabeling deep intuition as the height of vanity, as happened in the covid vaccine roll-out, doesn’t in any way change intuition’s actual nature. Calling wisdom ignorance won’t change wisdom’s nature either. Affirmations are a brilliant metaphysical technology, no matter how desperately corporations keep attempting to relabel them or replace them with their own indebting ersatz substitutes. It’s those crippling substitutes, including New Thought’s most distortedly ego-stoking and profitable versions of affirmation such as those peddled in The Secret, that deserve special warning and denunciation.

— Christophe

comments.title: Enlightenment, Human and Otherwise
comments.content:
In a previous discussion, you described various forms of enlightenment, upon which the soul would be ready to advance to the next level of being. For plants, enlightenment is experiencing themselves as an individual entity; and for animals, enlightenment is learning how to think about their own thoughts. Rather fascinating, and it makes sense.

So I'm wondering, can you provide any hints towards human enlightenment? Is it similar to the Buddhist path, eg Zen koans, or is it more like the Eternal Tao which cannot be told? Or is it truly knowing thyself, in the sense of getting to know the "real you" throughout your incarnations? I'm not expecting a complete explanation, which is a bit much to ask, just pointers.

Presumably, there are further enlightenments at the higher levels (spiritual/causal/divine) but that's too much to get into, a bit like teaching higher spatial dimensions to someone still figuring out arithmetic.

Since the lowest level of incarnation is crystals, I wonder what crystal enlightenment is like? Perhaps it's simply gaining sentience?

As always, thanks for hosting MM!

replies.title: Re: Enlightenment, Human and Otherwise
replies.content:
1) According to the teachings I follow, enlightenment at the human level involves awakening to the mental plane -- not the plane of chattering thoughts (those are astral phenomena), but the plane of meaning, value, and purpose. The sudden spasm that Eastern paths seek is not necessary, and can even become an obstacle; I think it was Robert Graves who described the pursuit of that experience for its own sake as the most refined form of masturbation imaginable.

The point is that as your mental sheath becomes a mental body and develops the first rudimentary organs of perception and action on the mental plane, you become aware of meaning, value, and purpose directly, rather than having to fumble around trying to grasp them on the astral; words stop being verbal noises and turn into constellations of meaning, and so on.

The mental body is also the first permanent body for our swarm of souls. Once the mental body forms out of the mental sheath, there is no further interruption of consciousness at death, and you're conscious of that -- this is why so many people who experience enlightenment say that it includes an awareness of your own immortality.

As for crystals, no, that's the point at which an etheric sheath begins to form, and the soul becomes aware of its own existence -- in place of a tumbling confusion of perceptions with no sense of someone perceiving them, there's the first stirrings of a sense of self. It's very faint and vague at that level, and it needs long ages of evolution through many forms to ripen.

replies.title: Re: Enlightenment, Human and Otherwise
replies.content:
Most excellent, ty!

re: Graves, in my readings of Buddhism, I came across a warning against seeking Enlightenment for its own sake, cautioning that this could become yet another obstacle and form of attachment, which is consistent.

As for human enlightenment, that's both more straightforward and more complex than I had thought. I guess it's not like there's some secret password written in an ancient scroll to go Super Saiyan -- instead, it's a long, arduous process of building up ones capacity on the mental plane. This ties in with an overall pattern of the occult, where ppl often desire shortcuts to power, leading only to folly; while the true path requires lots of work.

re: immortality, I can see how this ties into the surfacing of past life memories -- it's like the curtains of death are being drawn back.

re: crystals, so I see. I guess it's a very long path towards becoming fully conscious!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The most useful thing I can think of for you to do is to start turning all your vegetable waste, from your kitchen and yard, into compost, and working it into the soil. Most poor soil is poor because it doesn't have enough organic matter in it. You can get commercial compost and use that, but that's a poor second choice -- the quality of commercial compost is far lower than the stuff you can make yourself with a little home composter.

I'd also suggest, if you don't already say a daily rosary, get a rosary of wooden or green glass beads and start praying the rosary daily to the Virgin, asking her to bless the land. She's already shown that she's willing to do this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I suggest getting your soil analyzed to learn what mineral or minerals may be deficient or in excess. Adding compost for organic matter is important, but if your soil or the materials you are composting are deficient in a particular mineral or minerals or have too much of certain minerals, you will have difficulty growing certain plants in your garden. In other words, attend to material-plane issues of the soil as well as more subtle aspects.

Most state Extension services that I am aware of will accept soil samples and analyze them for a reasonable cost, but their recommendations may include amendments that would not be acceptable for organic gardens, if that is important to you. Logan Labs, loganlabs.com, accepts soil samples and analyzes them for about the same cost as my state Extension service, but it doesn't provide recommendations for what to add. For that, if you are growing plants for food, read the book The Intelligent Gardener: Growing Nutrient-Dense Food by Steve Solomon. If you want to grow ornamental plants, find out what minerals and pH they like to grow in and amend the soil accordingly; that's something your state Extension service or general gardening guides like The Garden Primer by Barbara Damrosch can provide.

replies.title: Re: q

Oddly enough, randomness appears (to me) to be a potent source of divinatory insight and magical effectiveness, at least in my own practice. (True randomness is almost impossible to obtain in real life, but close approximations to it are not all that hard: a well-shuffled deck of divinatory cards, for instance, or a well-shaken bag of runes.)

I doubt that any materialistic explanation of this power of randomness can ever be formulated. My own attempt to account for it is that in every effective act of divination or magic one has actually courted the cosmos in some subtle way, much as one might subtly court a human lover. In other words, the cosmos itself seems to be a vast sentient being, which -- oddly enough -- seems amenable to establishing relationships even with such ephemeral beings as we humans are. And successful magic or divination is one of its ways of responding to our courtship.

As Pico della Mirandola wisely wrote in the late 1400s, "To work magic is to marry the cosmos."

comments.title: The Pericón Cross - Protection Magic on the day of St. Michael Archangel
comments.content:
Today, September 29th is Michaelmas, or the Day of St. Michael Archangel and I thought of sharing a tradition from my hometown, the Pericon Cross. Pericón is the folk name for Tagetes lucida, a plant native to México and Central America and it a wildflower that grows on the side of roads or fields in much of the central part of the country and further down south. Its name in Náhuatl is Yauhtli.

This time of the year and until the end of dicember, a lot of things run wild and traditionally people have noticed that anger, misfortune, tragedy and accidents might happen among other things. In response to that, people that payed attention to the subtleties of this earth noticed that certain plants help field them away. One of such plants is pericón. It has a strong but floral and anise scent, from the family of the marigolds, that among many things helps to banish fear and depression when burned as incense, or bad luck and evil spirits when hung on the top of your door or the front of your car. There are many road accidents on these dates, for example.

The folk ritual of hanging a cross made of the plant is a rich syncretism of the catholic tradition and the wisdom of the earth that sprouted out of the Mesoamerican shamans and wisemen.

I don't know if pericón can be obtained outside of México, but if you can get ahold of a bunch, you can do it this way. If not, a cross or sacred symbol can be made out of another plant used for protection traditionally in these dates (or more generally) tying it in red string and hung on your main entrance to keep bad energy (and its carriers) at bay. This cross is consecrated to St. Michael Archangel and is hung on this day to reinforce its protective qualities. Done with devotion and prayed over with an incantation to St. Michael potentiates its effects considerably. Here are a couple pictures of the cross, as well as one of the periconeros, the farmers that grew it and gather it, sometimes as a family activity.

[IMAGE:0]
[IMAGE:1]

Interesting that both plants are yellow, strong scented, and grow on the sides of the road.

Other uses of it are burning it on charcoal, to remove etheric and physical pests; as well as taking a branch from the cross and incensing with it, or making tea, when there's been an argument at home to banish the trace of violence that tends to fester; and the fear or anger after it.

comments.title: Ecosophia Prayer List
comments.content:
At this page is the full list of all of the requests for prayer that have recently appeared at ecosophia.net and ecosophia.dreamwidth.org, as well as in the comments of the prayer list posts (printable version here, current to 9/30). Please feel free to add any or all of the requests to your own prayers.

If I missed anybody, or if you would like to add a prayer request for yourself or anyone who has given you consent (or for whom a relevant person holds power of consent) to the list, please feel free to leave a comment below.

* * *

This week I would like to bring special attention to the following prayer requests, selected from the fuller list.


May Mole End find help and insight in overcoming his vulnerable narcissism; may he find occasion to make amends to or otherwise bring healing and peace to all those that he has hurt and victimized, to the extent that they allow.

comments.title: Fraternal Lodge Design
comments.content:
Hey JMG,

I'm working through your book on designing a lodge and I'm attempting to map out the basic directional correspondences for a fraternity. I don't expect many people I'm running in circles with would be particularly interested in any kind of occult practice, but I think setting out proper, balanced correspondences makes sense as an organizational foundation, and I'm fascinated by the whole process.

For the lodge "myth" I'm focusing on the figure of Columbia, as the idealized spirit of America. The aim would be the development of civic minded, principled men, as the sons of Columbia, seeking to cultivate proper American virtues in an increasingly postmodern, nihilistic, atomistic age.

I wanted to use Venus, with the symbolism of the morning and evening star, to guide our calendar, and overlay the directional correspondences. I don't plan to incorporate any kind of astrological work with Venus, as such. The cycle of Venus' motion seemed convenient as a calendar, with lengthy but not overly long terms, novel (I don't think I've heard of using a Venusian calendar before), useful light and dark symbolic motifs associated with morning and evening star, and the feminine connection was aligned with Columbia in some general sense.

Because of the America theme, the "great work" is the "manifest destiny" -- and so I wanted that to be represented in the lodge opening, beginning in the east, but ending in the west - so E - S - N - W.

The West as the ultimate point would match the American journey, with the west as the furthest destination, and it also mirrors the western evening star ascent of Venus - with spirit below, above, within to round it out, like the SOP. There are two axes formed by the elemental correspondences, but also with the conjunctions of Venus and the Sun (N - S) and ascents (E - W). Seems like a good representation of the microcosm mythically/symbolically, but that doesn't necessarily mean it makes sense as a ritual act.

In your lodge book, you do talk about how various lodges set up different arrangements and movement patterns, but I wanted to get your thoughts.

- Any particular concerns for using a Venus based calendar?
- Any thoughts on the alternative: East, South, North, West directional movement in terms of a lodge opening / invocation/banishing ritual scheme?

Thank you!
Columbus

replies.title: Re: Fraternal Lodge Design
replies.content:
1) It rarely works well to use any calendar other than the one that the rest of society uses, since your members will need to fit in meetings around the rest of life. For example, lodges almost always "go dark" in July and August, since so many people are on vacation and halls tend to be too hot and stuffy for meetings then, and appropriate gaps for the Christmas holidays are also inevitable. You might want to consider instead simply using the symbolism.

2) Either one will work. The zigzag pattern is common in some occult lodges.
